Title: Khaled to meet Norwegian counterpart over blocked naval missile delivery
Article:
KLUANG: Defence Minister Datuk Seri Mohamed Khaled Nordin will meet his Norwegian counterpart in Singapore later this month to seek clarification on Norway's surprise decision to block the export licence for naval strike missiles (NSM) to Malaysia.
He said the meeting would also be used to express the Malaysian government's regret and surprise at the cancellation, which occurred just days before the scheduled delivery in March.
"It is very unfortunate as we signed the contract to purchase the missiles in 2018. They were supposed to be delivered in March to equip our littoral combat ships (LCS) as well as KD Lekiu and KD Jebat.
"But a few days before delivery, without any indication, the Norwegian government announced it would not grant the marketing licence after eight years. This is a surprise and it affects us, he said.
He was speaking to reporters after officiating the armed forces Madani Economic Programme here today.
Khaled said the action did not reflect good diplomatic relations and that Malaysia was currently using various channels, including ties between the respective prime ministers and foreign ministers, to resolve the crisis.
"Since this matter is still being handled through diplomacy, I do not want to comment further at this time," he said.
The Norwegian Foreign Ministry has reportedly withdrawn several export licences for certain technologies as part of efforts to tighten the country's export controls.
According to the Kongsberg Defence & Aerospace AS (KDA) website, the NSM procurement was signed between the Royal Malaysian Navy and KDA in April 2018. The contract was valued at 124 million euros (RM571.9 million) to equip six new LCS ships.
The missile is capable of long-range maritime strikes at high subsonic speeds with a range exceeding 300km.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Development impact more vital than total spending, says Hajiji
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Ensuring development reaches communities across Sabah is more important than simply exhausting the RM12.02 billion ceiling approved for the state.
Chief Minister Datuk Seri Hajiji Noor said the allocation, the largest under the 13th Malaysia Plan, was intended to fund 1,173 programmes and projects.
Based on past implementation challenges, he said the priority moving forward was to accelerate delivery, improve coordination, and ensure projects provided lasting value.
"Across Sabah, we are seeing strong momentum in infrastructure development, from roads and healthcare facilities to utilities and digital connectivity.
"The Pan Borneo Highway, in particular, captures this momentum. It represents far more than connectivity as it unlocks economic opportunities and improves access across the state.
"Our urban centres - Kota Kinabalu, Sandakan, and Tawau are evolving into modern, dynamic hubs that reflect the new Sabah we are building," he said at the opening ceremony of the International Construction Week (ICW) Borneo and BuildXpo at a hotel here.
His speech was delivered by Deputy Chief Minister and Finance minister Datuk Seri Masidi Manjun.
He said that over the next five years, the focus would centre on strategies, namely strengthening connectivity through transport networks, logistics systems, and port development to drive trade and economic expansion.
The other strategies are expanding access to affordable housing for the improvements in the lives of all Sabahans and raising industry standards through better governance, stronger capabilities, and greater accountability, he said.
In this respect, Hajiji said the ICW Borneo is an important platform that brings together ideas, expertise and partnerships that translate into real outcomes.
"It allows Sabah to engage directly with industry leaders, investors, and professionals who can be partners in our growth journey.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KTC unveils RM150mil industrial park blueprint in Sabah
Article:
KOTA KINABALU: Kim Teck Cheong Consolidated Bhd (KTC) has unveiled its long-term development blueprint for the RM150 million KTC Industrial Park at the Kota Kinabalu Industrial Park here.
The facility, to be developed on a newly acquired six-hectare site, is set to become the foundation of an integrated industrial ecosystem encompassing manufacturing, technology, trade, and distribution.
https://www.nst.com.my/business/corporate/2025/06/1229227/ktc-buys-rm40mil-plot-sabah-industrial-hub
Strategically positioned within KKIP, the industrial park is expected to enhance logistical efficiency while supporting KTC's long-term expansion plans across Sabah, Malaysia and the wider Asean market.
"When this land development proposal was first discussed five years ago, KTC made a firm commitment to the state government.
"This commitment was not merely about expanding business operations but, more importantly, about helping Sabah create job opportunities and actively contributing to improving the people's standard of living," according to a statement by KTC.
Although the project is slated for completion within 18 months, the company has continued to invest in local talent development and creating opportunities for Sabah's younger workforce.
The company is targeting to generate an additional 500 new job opportunities for locals as it expands its transport fleet by 400 vehicles and trucks.
"This will bring KTC's total workforce to nearly 2,300 employees, meaning more families supported, enhanced household economic stability, and a brighter future for the next generation of Sabahans."
KTC is also targeting growth of its annual revenue from RM1.3 billion to RM2 billion within the next three years, driven by the expansion of its industrial and logistics capabilities.
The groundbreaking ceremony of the KTC Industrial Park was officiated by Deputy Chief Minister III and Industrial Development, Entrepreneurship and Transport Minister B Datuk Ewon Benedick.
Also present were KTC chairman Tan Sri Mohd Zuki Ali and managing director Datuk Lau Kok Sing.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Yeo Bee Yin cautions timing of fuel subsidy cuts amid fragile economy
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Removing RON95 fuel subsidies for higher-income groups could trigger a broader economic downturn, DAP National Publicity Secretary Yeo Bee Yin warned.
Responding to Prime Minister Datuk Seri Anwar Ibrahim's announcement to cut subsidies for the T15 and T20 categories, she said the timing was detrimental to a fragile economy.
"The worst time to remove any subsidy is in the middle of a crisis when an expansionary fiscal policy, not a contractionary one, is required to sustain the economy," she said in a media statement issued at DAP headquarters today.
Yeo said fuel price increases would ripple through the supply chain. As many within the T15 and T20 categories are small business owners and employers, higher operating costs would likely be passed on to consumers.
"This creates a secondary wave of inflation for essential goods and services, ultimately hurting the very lower-income groups the government aims to protect," she said.
She said these groups drive domestic private consumption, and reducing their disposable income would affect the retail, hospitality and service sectors, worsening an outlook already burdened by the Middle East conflict.
She said the use of gross household income as a "blunt instrument" fails to account for geographical cost-of-living differences.
While a RM13,000 monthly income may be comfortable in rural areas, she said it represents the "struggling urban middle class" in the Klang Valley.
"A dual-income professional couple supporting children and elderly parents in the city has paper-thin disposable income. If the T15 threshold is implemented, nearly one-third of households in Selangor and Kuala Lumpur will be affected," she said.
She also flagged implementation risks, including petrol station bottlenecks and the emergence of a "grey market" where subsidised fuel could be illegally resold for profit.
She urged Putrajaya to provide data showing the move would not trigger a net economic loss before asking the public to bear the burden.
"Fiscal reform should not become a code word for squeezing the urban middle class to their breaking point. We should be strengthening our economy, not creating a self-inflicted cost-of-living crisis," she said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Bribery attempt fails as Maritime Malaysia detains overloaded sand dredger
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: An attempt to bribe enforcement officers was made after the Malaysian Maritime Enforcement Agency (MMEA) detained a sand dredger for carrying excess cargo off Tanjung Tokong yesterday.
The vessel was intercepted at 11.30pm by a patrol boat while anchored 1.0 nautical miles northeast of Tanjung Tokong following intelligence-led surveillance.
​​​​​​Despite the alleged bribery attempt and resistance encountered during the operation, officers proceeded with a full inspection of the vessel.
Penang Maritime director Captain Abd Razak Mohamed said the dredger was operated by 17 foreign crew members from Indonesia and China, aged between 32 and 52.
"Further checks revealed that several crew members failed to produce valid identification documents and are suspected to have entered Malaysia without passing through authorised entry points, believed to be offences under the Immigration Act 1959/63.
"The vessel was also found carrying sand beyond its permitted capacity, while additional individuals on board were not listed in the official crew manifest," he said today.
"During the inspection, there were also attempts to offer bribes to enforcement personnel and to obstruct officers from carrying out their duties, he added.
Following the operation, 10 out of the 17 crew members were detained and escorted to the Penang Maritime headquarters for further investigation.
The case is being investigated under the Immigration Act 1959/63, the Merchant Shipping Ordinance 1952, the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission Act 2009, and Section 186 of the Penal Code for obstructing public servants in the discharge of duty.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Senior govt agency executive remanded over RM8,000 bribery probe
Article:
KUANTAN: A senior construction executive at a government agency has been remanded for allegedly soliciting and accepting RM8,000 in bribes in 2024.
The bribes were allegedly received in exchange for helping secure contracts, approving contract evaluations, obtaining work completion certifications, and expediting payment claims for a construction company and a contractor.
The male suspect, in his 40s, was remanded for seven days until May 18 following an application by the Malaysian Anti-Corruption Commission (MACC) at the Kuantan Magistrate's Court today.
Magistrate Tengku Eliana Tuan Kamaruzaman granted the remand order.
According to a source, the suspect was detained around 4pm yesterday when he arrived at the Pahang MACC office to provide his statement.
"Investigations found that the suspect is believed to have committed the offences in 2024 by assisting both companies in securing contracts, approving contract evaluations, obtaining work completion certificates, and expediting payment claims.
"The suspect is believed to have received bribes from the companies through bank transfers into his personal account," the source said.
Meanwhile, Pahang MACC director Mohd Shukor Mahmud confirmed the arrest, adding that the case is being investigated under Section 16(a)(A) of the MACC Act 2009.
The offence is punishable under Section 24(1) of the same Act, which carries a maximum sentence of 20 years' imprisonment and a fine of no less than five times the value of the bribe or RM10,000, whichever is higher, upon conviction.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Tangkak murder: "I came to court to see my son's killer."
Article:
TANGKAK: "I came to court to see who killed my son," said Kim Ang, 60, the mother of Tong Wee Sheng, 38, who was hacked to death at a Bukit Kangkar restaurant two months ago.
Kim arrived at the court at 8am today with family after they were informed that
three suspects
linked to her son's death would be charged.
The grieving mother said she was suffering from insomnia and relied on medication to cope since the tragedy.
"I have not been able to sleep for two months. While I felt some relief when I learnt that the suspects had been arrested, it has not diminished my anger.
"I lost my son in an instant. It is too painful. My 11-year-old grandson constantly asks who killed his father."
Kim said she told the boy that the suspects had been caught to spare him further distress as he is still struggling to accept the loss.
"I do not know the accused, which is why we are here. I want to see their faces."
The victim's family carried banners with pleas for justice, including "return our son's life", "maximum punishment for the killers" and "justice for our son".
Kim said she knew the legal process would take time and hoped for an appropriate sentence.
She said her family was determined to follow every stage of the trial, even after the case is transferred to the Muar High Court.
"As long as the offenders have not been sentenced, our family will not be at peace.
"We are not seeking revenge, but justice. We want this to serve as a lesson to society."
During proceedings today, Pan Wei Siang, 24, Henry Lau Yi Sheng, 26, and Tan Ah Chai, 32, nodded to signify they understood the charge under Section 302 of the Penal Code read before magistrate Lee Kim Kiat.
The three were arrested on April 28 after hiding at several hotels in Melaka.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Johor Immigration arrests 33 foreigners in raids on 10 premises
Article:
JOHOR BARU: Johor Immigration Department enforcement officers arrested 33 undocumented foreigners in raids on 10 premises here yesterday.
Eight Malaysians suspected of harbouring the migrants were issued notices to assist investigations.
The 11am raids targeted 10 business premises, including eateries, car wash outlets, barber shops and shophouses used as hostels to house the foreigners.
Johor Immigration director Datuk Mohd Rusdi Mohd Darus said the operation targeted business premises suspected of employing and harbouring migrants illegally.
The 33 detainees, aged between 20 and 51, comprised a Nepalese man, four Indonesian men, four Indian men, two Bangladeshi men and 15 Myanmar men.
Also detained were two Indonesian women and five Myanmar women.
"The detainees were caught red-handed while working, while some were apprehended at their hostels," Rusdi said in a statement today.
All detainees are suspected of offences under the Immigration Act 1959/63 and Immigration Regulations 1963 for working without permits and overstaying. They are being held at the Setia Tropika Immigration Depot.
The offences carry fines, imprisonment of up to five years, caning and deportation.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Indonesian rupiah falls below 17,500/dollar for first time on US-Iran deadlock
Article:
JAKARTA: Indonesia's rupiah hit a record low below 17,500 per dollar on Tuesday, leading a broad pullback in emerging Asian currencies, as stalled US-Iran peace negotiations kept oil prices elevated and risk appetite subdued.
The Philippine peso dipped 0.8 per cent in its third straight session of losses, while the Indonesian rupiah weakened to an all-time low of 17,510 against the US dollar.
The peso and the rupiah have declined nearly 5 per cent and 7 per cent, respectively, since the war in the Middle East began in late February.
"In Asia, the Indonesian rupiah remains under pressure... as lingering energy disruptions raise the risk of fuel shortages," said MUFG senior currency analyst Lloyd Chan. "Indonesia's vulnerability is heightened by relatively low crude inventory buffers, largely due to storage capacity constraints."
The 10-week-long conflict in the Middle East has battered currencies across energy-reliant Asian economies, prompting central banks and governments to scramble and roll out measures to curb currency weakness, though with limited success.
Oil prices rose 1 per cent on Tuesday on sustained supply worries after US President Donald Trump said the ceasefire with Iran was "on life support," pointing to disagreements over several demands.
Indonesia's benchmark stock index shed as much as 1.5 per cent to its lowest since late June 2025, with heavy losses in utilities firm Barito Renewables and chemicals firm Chandra Asri Pacific.
Alongside energy shocks, Indonesia is grappling with concerns over fiscal discipline, central bank independence, and stock market regulation.
Bank Indonesia's governor assured markets last week that the central bank has sufficient foreign exchange reserves to make strong market interventions to stabilise the rupiah, if required.
Meanwhile, the Indian rupee hit an all-time low of 95.6250 on Tuesday, weighed down by expectations of prolonged high crude prices, persistent portfolio outflows and weakening sentiment.
MSCI's EM Asia equities gauge declined 0.5 per cent, dragged down by a 3.2 per cent drop in South Korea's tech-heavy KOSPI index. The benchmark climbed to a record high before pulling back sharply, as investors booked profits.
Chipmaker Samsung Electronics fell almost 4 per cent and peer SK Hynix declined more than 1 per cent.
Taiwan shares rose 1.1 per cent to an all-time peak and Malaysia's benchmark climbed 0.4 per cent ahead of first-quarter economic growth data on Friday.
Malaysia's economic growth likely eased in the first quarter, according to a Reuters poll, a week after the central bank stood pat on interest rates amid concerns about a slowdown and inflation from the prolonged conflict in the Middle East.
Philippine stocks slipped 0.3 per cent, with Jollibee Foods dropping almost 9 per cent to its lowest since October 2020 after a near 44 per cent plunge in first-quarter profit. The company also said it was reviewing certain 2026 targets.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Elderly woman deceived in phone scam loses RM327,600, land titles
Article:
KUANTAN: An elderly woman lost RM327,600 to scammers after being tricked into handing over two land titles as collateral to a moneylending company.
The 76-year-old housewife from Temerloh only realised she had been deceived after her son requested the land titles which were still registered under her late husband's name.
Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said the victim's ordeal began on May 28 last year when she received a phone call from an individual claiming to be a police officer from Sarawak.
"The caller claimed that the victim's MyKad had been misused by scammers and that her bank account was under investigation. She was instructed not to inform any family members.
"The suspect spoke in a harsh tone, causing the victim to feel afraid and comply with all his instructions. She was told to transfer money, allegedly for police investigation purposes," he said in a statement today.
Yahaya said the suspect assured the victim that the money would be refunded once the investigation was completed.
"The victim made 42 transactions totalling RM327,000 into seven bank accounts between May 28 last year and March 25 this year. The suspect also instructed her to pledge two of her land titles to a moneylender.
"The incident only came to light after the victim's son requested the land titles for estate distribution purposes as the land was still registered under the victim's late husband," he said.
Yahaya said the case is being investigated under Section 420 of the Penal Code for cheating, which carries a jail term of between one and 10 years, along with whipping and a fine upon conviction.
He advised the public to check bank account numbers at https://semakmule.rmp.gov.my before making any transactions.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Mechanic charged with stealing clothing, including ladies' underwear, from boutique
Article:
AYER KEROH: A 21-year-old mechanic was charged at the magistrate's court here today with stealing several items of clothing, including ladies' undergarments, from a boutique last week.
The accused, Muhammad Aidil Mustaqim Zulkifeli, pleaded not guilty after the charge was read before magistrate Uthman Abd Ghani.
He was alleged to have stolen nine items from a boutique in the AEON shopping mall at 7.50pm last Sunday.
The stolen goods, valued at RM491, included men's T-shirts, women's clothing, brassieres, shorts, and dresses.
The charge was framed under Section 380 of the Penal Code for theft in a building, which provides for a maximum 10-year prison sentence and a fine upon conviction.
Deputy public prosecutor Syauqi Mardia Baharudin offered bail at RM7,000.
However, National Legal Aid Foundation (YBGK) lawyer Muhamad Mawardi Zainudin, representing the accused, pleaded for a lower amount, citing his client's full cooperation during the investigation.
He said the accused earns approximately RM1,500 a month as a mechanic and supports his mother, who is suffering from brain cancer.
The court granted bail at RM2,500 with one surety and fixed June 9 for mention.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia's unemployment rate holds steady at 2.9pct in March 2026
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Malaysia's labour market remained stable in March 2026, with the unemployment rate unchanged at 2.9 per cent, supported by continued expansion in employment and labour force participation, according to the Statistics Department.
Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the positive economic outlook during the month enabled the country's labour market to sustain its steady growth momentum.
"The labour force increased by 0.1 per cent to 17.31 million persons in March 2026 from 17.30 million in February, while the labour force participation rate (LFPR) remained at 70.9 per cent," he said in a statement today.
Mohd Uzir said the number of employed persons also edged up by 0.1 per cent to 16.80 million persons compared with 16.79 million recorded in the previous month.
The department's Statistics of the Labour Force, Malaysia, March and First Quarter 2026 also show that the number of unemployed persons rose slightly by 0.4 per cent to 509,000 persons from 506,800 in February, leaving the unemployment rate unchanged at 2.9 per cent.
The chief statistician said employees continued to make up the largest share of total employment at 75 per cent, increasing marginally to 12.60 million persons in March from 12.59 million previously.
"On the same note, the number of own-account workers recorded a 0.3 per cent rise to 3.14 million persons compared with 3.13 million in February," he added.
Mohd Uzir said the services sector remained the main contributor to employment growth, particularly in accommodation and food and beverage services, information and communication activities, as well as transportation and storage activities.
"The manufacturing, construction and agriculture sectors also recorded increases in employment, while the mining and quarrying sector saw a slight decline," he added.
On unemployment, Mohd Uzir said actively unemployed persons, defined as those available for work and actively seeking jobs, accounted for 79.7 per cent of total unemployed persons. This category increased by 0.3 per cent to 405,800 persons in March from 404,700 in February.
At the state level, Mohd Uzir said the Federal Territory of Putrajaya recorded the lowest unemployment rate at 1.3 per cent, followed by Pahang at 1.9 per cent, while Selangor and Melaka each posted two per cent.
He said Putrajaya also registered the highest LFPR at 79.2 per cent, followed by Selangor at 78 per cent and the Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur at 76 per cent.
Looking ahead, Mohd Uzir said Malaysia's labour market is expected to remain stable, supported by strong domestic fundamentals and ongoing structural transformation.
"However, growth is likely to be more moderate and increasingly shaped by external developments, particularly the trajectory of the global energy crisis and geopolitical conditions.
"As such, both businesses and the workforce will need to remain agile in navigating a more dynamic and uncertain economic environment," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Gold could surge to US$5,600 by end-2026 amid global uncertainty, says UBS
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Gold's rally is set to extend further, with prices potentially reaching as much as US$5,600 (about RM21,957.90) or RM705.96 per gram by the end of 2026 amid persistent global uncertainty, according to UBS Investment Bank.
Its precious metals strategist Joni Teves said the outlook is supported by heightened geopolitical uncertainty, persistent inflation pressures and expectations of lower interest rates over the coming years.
She said the bullish trajectory remains intact even as gold undergoes a period of consolidation following strong gains in recent years.
Teves added that UBS continues to expect gold to reach new highs this year, with upside risks to its forecasts increasing over the medium to long term as diversification into the metal gathers pace among both institutional and official sector investors.
"We maintain our positive view on gold and still see prices recovering from current levels to print new highs this year," she said during a virtual media briefing on UBS's gold and precious metals outlook.
Teves said the key driver remains strong demand from both institutional and official sectors, as gold becomes an increasingly core portfolio asset amid persistent macroeconomic uncertainty.
She noted that while UBS had previously expected the current gold bull cycle to peak towards the end of this year and next year, rising risks from higher energy prices and ongoing geopolitical tensions in the Middle East have tilted risks to the upside.
"If global growth slows and the US Federal Reserve turns more dovish than currently expected, it could extend the gold upcycle over the coming years," she said.
She added that the market is still in a wait-and-see phase, assessing how inflation and growth evolve alongside rising energy costs.
Near-term price consolidation is expected to continue, but Teves said this could present opportunities for investors to build positions.
"We think the market remains underinvested for now and would therefore view any pullback to test the US$4,000 psychological level as an opportunity to build positions at better levels," she said.
According to UBS, central bank buying continues to provide structural support to gold, helping absorb supply and stabilise prices during periods of macroeconomic stress.
Teves said fears that central banks might sell gold due to geopolitical shifts or oil price volatility have not materialised at scale.
"Latest World Gold Council data shows official sector buying in the first quarter was slightly higher than last year," she said, adding that diversification into gold reserves is likely to continue amid global uncertainty.
Physical demand also remains supportive, particularly in Asia, even as jewellery demand softens due to high prices.
"Sentiment in China remains positive, and investment demand in the first two months of the year was very strong," she said, noting that concerns over currency weakness and macro uncertainty are supporting physical inflows.
On silver, UBS remains bullish, expecting it to outperform gold due to its higher volatility and "high beta" characteristics.
Investor sentiment towards silver has improved significantly, with UBS expecting demand to remain more tactical compared with the strategic allocation typically seen in gold.
Strong physical demand from China is also expected to provide additional support, particularly from restocking and investment flows, UBS said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Russia, Ukraine resume strikes as truce expires, one dead
Article:
KYIV: Russia resumed strikes on Ukraine as a three-day truce expired on Tuesday, attacking the capital with drones and killing one person in the eastern region of Dnipropetrovsk, Ukrainian authorities said.
The Russian military meanwhile said it had shot down 27 Ukrainian drones after the ceasefire expired.
US President Donald Trump had announced the truce on Friday, hours before Russia's World War II victory celebrations, saying he hoped it would mark "the beginning of the end" of the four-year-old conflict.
But even before it expired, the two countries had traded accusations of attacks on civilians that violated the truce.
As the ceasefire ended on Tuesday, Kyiv came under drone attacks, according to Ukrainian authorities.
"Enemy UAVs are currently over Kyiv. Please stay safe until the alert is cleared," the head of the capital's military administration, Tymur Tkachenko, posted on Telegram.
The alert siren was the first confirmed in the capital since Friday, before the ceasefire came into force.
Kyiv's regional military administration told residents to remain in shelters and said its air defences could be operating in the area.
Russian strikes in eastern Ukraine killed one person and wounded at least four others, regional military authorities said.
The strikes killed a man and wounded a woman in the area of Synelnykove in the Dnipropetrovsk region, the head of the regional military administration Oleksandr Ganzha posted on Telegram.
He added three others were wounded in strikes elsewhere in Dnipropetrovsk.
On the Russian side, "air defence duty assets intercepted and destroyed 27 Ukrainian fixed-wing unmanned aerial vehicles" over the Belgorod, Voronezh and Rostov regions from midnight to 7 am Moscow time (2100 to 0400 GMT), Russia's defence ministry said in a statement.
Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy had said Monday that fighting with Russia was ongoing despite the truce, accusing Moscow of not wanting to end the war started by President Vladimir Putin's February 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
"Today there was no silence at the front, there was fighting. We have recorded all of this," Zelenskyy said in his daily address in the final hours of the truce.
Zelenskyy also said it was "clear that the war in Iran is now drawing the most attention from America."
Negotiations on the Russia-Ukraine war have so far led nowhere, and have been largely sidelined by the Iran conflict – though Trump's ceasefire announcement had raised some hope that US-led talks to end Russia's invasion could be resumed.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: KL flood ponds lost 70pct capacity after land transferred to developers - Yeoh
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR:  The capacity of flood retention ponds in Jinjang and Batu have shrunk to about 30 per cent of their original size after 17 land lots were transferred to developers since 2015.
Minister in the Prime Minister's Department (Federal Territories) Hannah Yeoh said about 70 per cent of the land area within the water body zones had been transferred for development purposes, reducing the ponds' original size of 33.5 hectares.
She said a technical assessment by the Irrigation and Drainage Department found that the situation had affected the original function of the flood retention ponds and made maintenance works, as well as machinery access, difficult.
"The government cannot continue to allow development that compromises the function of flood retention ponds.
"The interests of the people and the safety of city residents must be prioritised even if the measures taken require firm decisions to rectify long-inherited problems," she said in a statement.
Yeoh said the Federal Territories Department had established a task force chaired by its director-general Datuk Azmi Mohd Zain to strengthen coordination and flood mitigation efforts in Kuala Lumpur.
She added that Kuala Lumpur City Hall (DBKL) would not approve planning permission for any development applications on flood retention pond land until all conditions set by Drainage and Irrigation Department were fully complied with.
She said the move was aimed at ensuring the ponds' original function and maintenance access were preserved.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Xi-Trump talks spotlight contrasting styles, expectations
Article:
DONALD Trump has said he expects a "big, fat hug" from China's Xi Jinping when they meet in Beijing this week, though multiple thorny issues between the two sides could see the US president kept at arm's length.
Taiwan, tariffs, rare earths and the war in Iran are among the contentious topics set to be discussed by the two leaders, whose styles of communication could hardly be more different.
Trump's often bombastic, freewheeling comportment on the international stage contrasts dramatically with Xi's measured, tight-lipped approach to foreign policy.
In mid-April, the US president predicted that Xi would greet him with a "big, fat hug" upon his arrival in Beijing, adding that "we are working together smartly, and very well!"
China, in contrast, did not confirm the visit was taking place until Monday, typical of its reserve on such matters.
Despite the clash of styles, Beijing's foreign ministry said Monday that China considers leader-to-leader diplomacy as playing an "irreplaceable strategic guiding role in China-US relations."
Trump's trip to China, set to begin Wednesday, is the first by a US president since his own in 2017.
During that first-term sojourn, he received what the Chinese termed "state visit-plus" treatment, including a private tea reception in the Forbidden City.
His 2026 trip is expected to include a tour of the Temple of Heaven and a state banquet, but analysts say the pomp will not match that of nine years ago.
Since Trump's first visit, China has learned that while "aggrandisement... plays to his ego, it cannot prevent him from a quick flip-flop in his attitude towards China", Wu Xinbo, director of the Center for American Studies at Shanghai's Fudan University, told AFP.
In 2017, Chinese state media presented "personal diplomacy as opening a new phase of pragmatic cooperation and great power diplomacy", wrote the National University of Singapore's Bert Hofman in a newsletter on Monday.
But Trump's subsequent anti-China policy tack over the course of his first term, followed by a blistering trade war at the beginning of his second, disabused Beijing of that notion.
"The revived Trump-era discourse in 2025-2026 is less romantic about leader chemistry (in contrast to Trump's continued mentioning of his friendship with Xi)," Hofman wrote.
While there is no longer hope the two men's personal relationship can be transformative, there is acknowledgement it could "prevent derailment, restart channels, and produce tactical bargains", he said.
Beijing has rolled out the red carpet for a broad swathe of other foreign guests recently.
Russian President Vladimir Putin and North Korean leader Kim Jong Un were among the attendees to be warmly welcomed at a military parade in Beijing in September.
And French President Emmanuel Macron got significant facetime with Xi both in the capital and in the southwestern city of Chengdu.
But some analysts think the connection between the leaders of the world's two largest economies is unforced.
John Gong, professor at the University of International Business and Economics in Beijing, told AFP he thought Trump and Xi "really do get along very well."
"I think they genuinely have some kind of a warm relationship towards each other," he said.
Whether that personal relationship trumps the many sore points in the bilateral one remains doubtful.
Trump, who will turn 80 next month, and Xi, who will turn 73 a day later, last met face-to-face in October on the sidelines of a regional summit in South Korea.
There, the two agreed to a one-year truce in a trade war that saw tariffs on many goods exceed 100 per cent.
This week's talks will be overshadowed by the US-Israel war in Iran, the most recent addition to a long roster of issues on which the two sides find themselves at loggerheads.
Expectations for outcomes this time should be "modest", said Wendy Cutler, a trade diplomat and vice president of the Asia Society Policy Institute, in the Geoeconomic Competition podcast last week.
"This year, we may see a number of meetings between Presidents Trump and Xi Jinping, and so all the deliverables don't have to be announced at the first meeting," she said.
"For each leader, stabilisation makes sense in the relationship," she added.
* The writer is from AFP
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Ekuinas makes RM7.1b in Bumiputera equity value
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Ekuiti Nasional Bhd (Ekuinas) has generated Bumiputera equity value of RM7.1 billion and total shareholder value of RM8.5 billion since its inception, supported by an investment allocation of RM5.1 billion across 49 portfolio companies.
This performance was backed by cumulative gains of RM5.7 billion, alongside a strengthened strategic portfolio — including the healthcare sector, which increased to 29 per cent of Ekuinas' active portfolio in 2025.
Its proportion of Bumiputera employees in management positions within Ekuinas' portfolio companies rose to 36.9 per cent.
Ekuinas has released its 2025 Impact Report, marking a year of transition and positive progress as the government-linked company strengthened its role within Malaysia's investment ecosystem.
It said the year reflects a more integrated approach to delivering both financial performance and socio-economic outcomes.
It added that private equity remains the core of its strategy, focusing on transforming high-potential companies into market leaders through active ownership.
Ekuinas said that through an initial public offering (IPO) and share transfer exercise, it realised gross proceeds of RM828 million, RM350 million of which were distributed as dividends to Permodalan Nasional Bhd (PNB) for the benefit of its unit trust holders.
"The transaction also marked the first Bumiputera 'Relay Race', with a 60 per cent stake in Orkim Bhd transitioning into long-term institutional ownership under PNB, to support the company's next phase of growth."
Ekuinas said its RM800 million private credit arm has completed its first two transactions, providing syariah-compliant, bespoke financing solutions tailored to underserved mid-market companies.
Its chief executive officer, Aliff Omar Mohamad Omar, said the company's progress in 2025 reflects its continued evolution as an integrated private capital investor, supporting Malaysian mid-market businesses across various stages of growth.
"We remain aligned with national priorities, such as GEAR-UP, while maintaining our discipline in delivering sustainable long-term returns and impact."
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia weighs cutting RON95 subsidy cap to 150 litres
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia is weighing a further tightening of its subsidised RON95 fuel allocation under the BUDI95 programme, with the monthly cap potentially reduced to 150 litres as part of broader efforts to better manage fuel supply, improve subsidy efficiency and reduce leakage.
Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong said the proposed adjustment is based on consumption data showing that a large majority of Malaysians use significantly less than the current subsidised threshold.
The quota was previously reduced from 300 litres to 200 litres in March as part of the initial rationalisation phase.
"The data consistently show that 80 per cent of Malaysians use less than 200 litres. The next step is 150 litres [as] 60 per cent of the population uses less than 150 litres," he said during a fireside chat at the Affin Market Outlook 2026: Propelling Malaysia Forward event on Tuesday.
Liew added that the mechanism should not only be viewed from a fiscal perspective but also as part of a broader strategy to ensure long-term fuel supply resilience, especially amid geopolitical uncertainties and global energy shocks.
"The number one concern for the nation is how to ensure that we have supply and consistent supply for as long as possible, even if the war drags on," he said.
The government previously spent about RM700 million monthly on fuel subsidies before recent global tensions, but the figure has since surged to RM7 billion a month.
Despite the rising subsidy bill, Liew stressed that ensuring affordable fuel access for lower-income groups, particularly motorcycle riders and the precarious workforce, remains a priority.
"Politically, I want to ensure that motorcycle users continue to enjoy a highly subsidised rate because if the precarious class are affected by high prices, you may have to pay a high political and societal price," he said.
On broader structural reforms, Liew said Malaysia must rethink its heavy reliance on private vehicles and redesign urban planning policies to encourage greater public transport use and inner-city living.
He said the current highway business model, where operators benefit from traffic congestion, was no longer sustainable under subsidy reforms such as BUDI95.
"There is a price tag that the finance ministry has to pay for every litre of oil wasted in congestion.
"What is needed is political understanding as well as consensus among the elites and the public so that more people stay in inner cities in the long run, fewer people need cars and more people use public transport, although it will take time to see the results," he added.
Liew also pointed to China's long-term investment in electric vehicles and electrification as an example of how nations address strategic vulnerabilities and energy security concerns.
Beyond fuel reforms, he said Malaysia must move away from a "just-in-time efficiency model" towards a more resilient "just-in-case" approach to strengthen national economic security.
He said the country needs to build greater circularity within the domestic economy to reduce vulnerabilities during global disruptions such as wars, pandemics and supply chain shocks.
"We are a very open economy, but we are so open that we actually do not like to talk to our neighbours because they do not pay us enough.
"We like to import from China and export to Europe because Europe pays the top dollar while China gives us the cheapest input.
"But that sort of model will get us into trouble when the next war happens, the next pandemic happens or another crisis emerges," said Liew.
He added that Malaysia must strengthen domestic economic circularity through tax incentives and be willing to accept slightly higher costs in exchange for more secure supply chains and guaranteed off-taking arrangements.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: BUDI95 a key tool in managing fuel demand: Liew
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Budi Madani RON95 (BUDI95) initiative is seen as a useful demand management tool that helps Malaysia understand national usage patterns, said Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong.
He noted that under the initiative, introduced in September last year, each person was initially allocated 300 litres per month, before it was reduced to 200 litres per month.
"I think the nation's number one concern is how to ensure that we have a consistent supply for as long as possible, even if the war (in Middle East) drags on," he said.
He added that clear communication and securing broad public support to reduce consumption as much as possible while ensuring long-term supply could help address the fiscal issue to some extent.
Liew said this during a fireside chat titled 'Malaysia's Response to Global Energy Crisis: Build Back Better', moderated by The Edge's assistant editor Emir Zainul
Ensuring continuous access to fuel supply, even at reduced levels, is important, as political and social stability will enable Malaysia to manage the crisis more confidently, he said.
He emphasised that Malaysia is entering the crisis from a position of relative strength, noting that while many countries have seen downward revisions in growth projections after February, Malaysia's outlook remains resilient.
"Malaysia's economic growth consensus is expected to be between 4.5 and 5.0 per cent (as announced by Bank Negara Malaysia), higher than the government's projection of 4.0-4.5 per cent announced last October.
"We are entering this crisis in a position of strength. However, we must be mindful that this crisis is likely to last longer than initially expected," he said.
He added that Malaysia must adopt a build-back-better approach by first defining the desired post-crisis end state and then working backwards to determine the steps required to achieve it.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Petronas LNG, MISC ink 20-year charter deal for five new LNG carriers
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Petronas LNG Ltd has signed a 20-year time charter agreement with MISC Group for five newbuild 174,000-cbm liquefied natural gas carriers, strengthening its long-term supply reliability while supporting the energy transition towards a lower-carbon future.
The agreement was signed by Petronas LNG chief executive officer Ezran Mahadzir and MISC vice president of Gas Asset and Solutions Hazrin Hasan.
It follows the conclusion of shipbuilding contracts between MISC and Hudong-Zhonghua Shipbuilding (Group) Co Ltd in January and February 2026.
The liquefied natural gas (LNG) carriers will be constructed in Shanghai, China, with charter commencement expected between 2029 and 2030.
Petronas executive vice president and chief executive officer of gas and maritime business Datuk Adif Zulkifli said the addition of these new LNG carriers marks another important milestone as the group continues leveraging the collective strengths of its businesses to create long-term value across the LNG value chain.
"By aligning our growth ambitions with MISC's maritime expertise, we are strengthening the integrated capabilities that support Petronas' position as a trusted and reliable global LNG supplier," he said in a statement today.
The vessels will incorporate modern and efficiency-driven technologies aligned with evolving environmental and operational standards.
These include the latest XDF2.1 propulsion technology, shaft generators that enhance fuel efficiency during voyages, and an onboard reliquefaction plant to effectively manage boil-off.
MISC will provide project management services throughout the shipbuilding phase, followed by operationalisation and ship management of the vessels upon delivery beginning 2029.
This integrated approach ensures greater operational continuity and reliability from construction to commercial operations, leveraging MISC's extensive experience in LNG vessel management.
The collaboration reflects Petronas' continued focus on strengthening the resilience and reliability of its LNG supply chain through strategic partnerships while contributing to the advancement of Malaysia's energy and maritime capabilities.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: MetMalaysia issues thunderstorm warning for nine states until 3pm
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Thunderstorms, heavy rain, and strong winds are expected to persist across several areas in nine states until 3pm today.
In a weather warning issued at noon, the Malaysian Meteorological Department (MetMalaysia) said the affected areas in Kedah include Langkawi, Sik, and Baling, while the Barat Daya and Timur Laut districts in Penang are also on alert.
In Perak, the warning covers Larut, Matang and Selama, Hulu Perak, Kuala Kangsar, Manjung, Kinta, Perak Tengah, Kampar, Bagan Datuk, Hilir Perak, and Batang Padang.
Similar conditions are forecast for Jeli in Kelantan, Cameron Highlands in Pahang, and the Sabak Bernam, Kuala Selangor, and Klang districts in Selangor.
In Johor, the warning involves Kota Tinggi and Johor Baru, while Miri in Sarawak is also affected.
In Sabah, the alert covers Nabawan in the Interior division, Kota Kinabalu and Tuaran on the West Coast, and Tawau, Kunak, and Semporna in the Tawau division.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia–China to invest RM500mil in high TVET push for EV and advanced tech talent
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia and China will allocate more than RM500 million to develop students under High Technical and Vocational Education and Training (High TVET) through the Malaysia–China Institute (MCI).
Deputy Prime Minister Datuk Seri Ahmad Zahid Hamidi said the collaboration between Malaysia and China includes the provision of various advanced technology equipment for learning and training purposes.
Zahid said the collaboration is aimed at preparing Malaysian students for emerging industries such as electric vehicles (EVs), battery technology and advanced automotive systems, as the global manufacturing landscape rapidly evolves towards new technologies.
He said Malaysia is shifting its TVET ecosystem beyond conventional education towards high TVET, which focuses on advanced technological skills and industry-driven training.
"If we do not prepare from now, our students will be left behind in terms of employability in these new technology sectors," he said.
Zahid, who is also the rural and regional development minister, spoke after launching the Malaysia–China Forum on EV, battery and new energy talent development and innovation today.
He said Malaysia aims to create nearly 500,000 job opportunities in high-technology sectors by 2030, while aiming to further enhance workforce employability by 2050.
He said cooperation with China in TVET and emerging technologies has been ongoing since 2023, with China offering 5,125 training placements to Malaysian TVET students for exposure to new technological fields.
"All the places offered last year were fully utilised, and we are now waiting for a new intake this year," he said.
As part of the bilateral commitment, he said Malaysia is strengthening the MCI as a specialised institution focused on high TVET and advanced technologies.
He said the institute is expected to expand beyond its current facilities and complement existing technical institutions such as the German-Malaysian Institute (GMI) and Malaysia-Japan Industrial Institute (MJII).
"However, the collaboration with China is different because it focuses specifically on high TVET and the latest technologies," he said.
Zahid said stronger coordination involving the Education Ministry, Higher Education Ministry, National TVET Council and 12 related ministries would further deepen collaboration with China, particularly in vocational education and engineering.
He added that the partnership would not only involve technology development and knowledge sharing but also provide Malaysian students with greater opportunities for industrial exposure and further studies in China.
Beyond vehicle assembly, the cooperation is also expected to include parts manufacturing and the production of industrial equipment required by Chinese industries, including EV-related products and other sectors.
"They require highly skilled workers in the sectors they are focusing on, and that is what we are developing," he said.
Zahid said China is also supporting Malaysia through the contribution of training equipment and technology tools for TVET institutions.
He said Malaysia has already received new EV vehicles for training purposes, with more learning equipment expected to arrive in the near future.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Seven gig platforms yet to submit worker data to Perkeso
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Seven major gig platform providers have yet to submit workers' data to the Social Security Organisation (Perkeso) despite the enforcement of the Gig Workers Act 2025 (Act 872).
Perkeso chief executive officer Datuk Seri Dr Mohammed Azman Aziz Mohamed said of 14 platform providers that they engaged, seven had come forward to share the necessary information.
"The Act requires all gig workers to register and contribute under the social security protection scheme.
"We have received cooperation from several providers, but there are two important matters that need to be addressed.
"First is the sharing of workers' data so we can register them in our system. If accidents, injuries or deaths occur, we will have the records needed to process assistance and claims.
"Secondly, through system integration, contributions can be automatically deducted from workers' earnings and channelled directly into the system," he told reporters.
He said this after visiting the e-hailing driver, Jalani Sapii, involved in the fatal accident on the MEX Highway involving military officers and a Bangladeshi tourist.
Mohammed Azman said the seven platform providers that had submitted workers' data to Perkeso were Airasia Ride Sdn. Bhd.; Kiddocare; Eternal Meteor Sdn Bhd (GOGET); ShopeeFood Malaysia; Troopers Innovation Sdn Bhd; Fastgig Sdn Bhd and Delivery HeroMalaysia (Foodpanda) Sdn Bhd.
He said FastGig was currently the only platform provider that had completed both the data integration and contribution deduction processes.
"The others should expedite the process because it is unfair to gig workers if accidents or deaths happen and we have no records to assist them."
Mohammed Azman, however, declined to name the seven remaining platform providers that had yet to submit workers' information.
He said the target deadline should have been before the enforcement of the Gig Workers Act on March 31.
"The welfare of workers is important. Once the Act came into force, the records should immediately have been with us.
On the case involving Jalani, Mohammed Azman said Perkeso had independently initiated assistance through its Skuad Prihatin based on existing records under the Act.
Jalani said the benefits he received from Perkeso had greatly helped ease his financial burden while he was unable to work following the accident.
"Alhamdulillah, the benefits I received from Perkeso have been very helpful, especially with the expenses I've incurred while I'm unable to work," he said.
He said he was currently receiving temporary disability benefits under the self-protection scheme, with the amount depending on the duration stated in his medical certificate (MC).
"The number of MC days determines the amount Perkeso pays for temporary disability. So far, I have only claimed for temporary disability," he said, adding that he was given 41 days of medical leave.
Jalani said doctors diagnosed him with a crack in the upper fibula of his left leg.
"After the 41 days, I will go for another check-up and if the doctor gives me the all-clear, I will return to work," he said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Four decades on, Sungai Kreh pollution still unresolved
Article:
GEORGE TOWN: Pollution in Sungai Kreh remains a persistent environmental concern despite multiple enforcement actions, with seven complaints involving water contamination and foul odour recorded between 2024 and 2026.
State Local Government Committee chairman H'ng Mooi Lye said the river continues to fall under the polluted to moderately polluted category based on monitoring conducted under the Environmental Quality Monitoring Programme.
Replying to a question from Mohd Sobri Saleh (Pas-Permatang Berangan) at the state legislative assembly sitting here today, H'ng said assessments conducted through three manual stations and one station at Sungai Air Melintas reflected ongoing water quality deterioration despite coordinated intervention by enforcement agencies.
"Investigations by the Department of Environment (DOE) found clogged drainage areas with excessive water hyacinth growth, contributing to slow water flow and foul odour. Water samples were collected and analysed by the Chemistry Department," he said.
It was previously reported that thousands of residents in Kampung Selamat, especially those living along Sungai Kreh, have endured nearly four decades of foul odour and water pollution believed to be linked to pig farming activities.
H'ng said enforcement had shown marginal improvement, with non-compliant premises declining from 48 per cent in 2024 to 43 per cent in 2025, but admitted the reduction had yet to translate into meaningful improvements in river conditions.
He said pollution was not limited to industrial sources, but also stemmed from markets, livestock operations and agricultural activities, complicating enforcement efforts.
Despite continuous monitoring by the DOE, Drainage and Irrigation Department (DID), Seberang Prai City Council (MBSP) and Veterinary Services Department (DVS), H'ng said enforcement constraints remained a major challenge in achieving lasting results.
The DVS, he added, continues enforcement through inspections, audits and sampling, but acknowledged limitations in manpower and logistics that have hindered broader coverage and impact.
To address the issue at source, H'ng said the state was proposing a structural intervention involving the transfer of pig waste to the Ampang Jajar Transfer Station for treatment via anaerobic digestion, converting organic waste into methane for renewable energy.
Despite the ongoing challenges, he said the state government remained committed to resolving longstanding contamination issues linked to pig farming activities.
"This administration remains committed to tackling environmental concerns, including water pollution and foul odour, with relevant agencies directed to intensify monitoring, enforcement and mitigation efforts," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia fast-tracks semiconductor listings on Bursa to align market with industry growth
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Finance Ministry is beefing up efforts to reshape Malaysia's capital market by establishing a clearer and more structured pathway for semiconductor-related companies to list on Bursa Malaysia, in a strategic push to align the equity market more closely with the country's fast-growing industrial and technology sectors.
Deputy Finance Minister Liew Chin Tong said discussions were already underway with the Securities Commission managing director Datin Paduka Azalina Adham and Bursa Malaysia Bhd chief executive officer Datuk Fad'l Mohamed.
"We are trying to look at how to create a pathway for more semiconductor-related companies to be listed in the domestic capital market," he said in his keynote address at Affin Market Outlook 2025 today.
Liew said the focus includes multinational semiconductor firms operating in Malaysia and locally grown companies within the broader semiconductor ecosystem.
He noted that Malaysia's stock market does not fully reflect the country's industrial strengths, as it remains shaped by a long-standing reliance on a foreign direct investment-driven economic model.
"Because we have been very dependent on a foreign direct investment-driven model. It has always been about FDI bringing capital into the country, and they don't circulate or recycle capital domestically," said Liew.
He added that strengthening the domestic capital market ecosystem would be an important step towards building a more resilient Malaysian economy amid the ongoing global AI and semiconductor boom.
Liew pointed to regional benchmarks such as South Korea and Taiwan, saying Malaysia should position itself as a smaller but meaningful player in the global semiconductor value chain.
"To build a stronger nation in a crisis and out of a crisis It is about building secure people.
"We may be smaller, but we can still develop a strong ecosystem, almost like a mini-Korea or mini-Taiwan in this cycle of tech and AI growth," he said.
Liew also highlighted that Malaysia already holds niche strengths in the semiconductor supply chain, particularly in equipment automation and supporting services, even if these segments are less visible in terms of valuation compared to leading global hubs.
He said expanding listings in these areas would help deepen domestic capital formation and strengthen linkages between industry and the financial market.
"We will have to think about jobs, wages, and housing as well as how we run our cities in order so that we can create people who are confident, who are economically secure and who are able to confidently participate in Malaysia's potential second take-off," added Liew.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: CME urges major subsidy overhaul, proposes shift to voucher-based support model
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Malaysia should overhaul its rigid, tiered subsidy framework and replace it with a more flexible, market-based voucher system to improve targeting and reduce economic distortions, the Centre for Market Education (CME) said.
The think tank argued that current subsidy mechanisms risk misallocating support by relying heavily on income classifications while also distorting market prices at the point of sale.
It said that while government support for households facing cost-of-living pressures is justified, the delivery mechanism is critical in ensuring efficiency and fairness.
CME stressed that income alone is not an adequate measure of need, noting that households with similar earnings can face very different financial realities depending on commuting distance, dependents, regional costs, family obligations and work arrangements.
"For example, one person earning RM8,000 may work from home and have limited transport costs, while another person earning the same income may travel long distances every day or support several dependents. Treating these two individuals in the same way because their income is similar misses the point. Need is not captured by income alone," Dr Carmelo Ferlito, chief executive officer of CME, said.
He said subsidies should not distort market prices at the point of sale nor rely solely on broad income categories that often fail to reflect real household circumstances.
"Malaysia needs a subsidy system that protects those who genuinely need help without undermining price signals." Dr Ferlito said, adding that the issue is not the intention to support people but the design of a system that is too rigid, too bureaucratic and too detached from the real household circumstances.
CME proposed maintaining a single, market-based price for subsidised goods such as fuel, while channelling assistance directly to eligible individuals through purpose-specific vouchers for essentials including fuel, transport, food and other basic needs.
It said a single market price would preserve transparency, reduce confusion at the point of sale, limit abuse and maintain the economic function of prices, while vouchers would allow assistance to be delivered in a more targeted and flexible manner.
"The key principle is simple. The market price should remain one, while support should be individualised. Once we start creating different prices for different people, we create administrative complexity, distortions, and incentives for manipulation," Dr Ferlito said.
CME also suggested that vouchers could be made tradable within a regulated framework, allowing recipients who do not fully utilise their assistance to transfer it to those with greater need.
A voucher market can help reveal real needs better than a centralised formula, Dr Ferlito said.
"If someone is eligible for assistance but does not need the full amount, the ability to sell or transfer the voucher allows the support to move toward those who value it more. This is not a weakness of the system; it is precisely how flexibility can improve targeting: the market can do a better job than a centralised approach."
He said such a mechanism would combine social protection with market-based allocation, enabling more accurate distribution of assistance based on individual circumstances rather than broad classifications.
He added that vouchers should be purpose-specific, digitally traceable, and supported by safeguards against fraud, hoarding, and abuse, while cautioning against excessive restrictions that could undermine their flexibility.
CME also noted that a voucher system would make the fiscal cost of support more transparent.
Unlike generalised subsidies, which often hide their true cost and disproportionately benefit higher-consuming households, vouchers would allow the government to define the size of the transfer, identify the intended beneficiaries, and evaluate the effectiveness of the policy.
"General subsidies are fiscally expensive, socially imprecise, and economically distortive. A purpose-voucher system is not perfect, but it is a better institutional compromise. It allows the government to help people without pretending that prices can be politically engineered without consequences," Dr Ferlito said.
The think tank said Malaysia's subsidy reform should be guided by three principles – one market price, targeted support through purpose-vouchers, and flexibility in the allocation of assistance based on actual needs rather than rigid classifications.
"The goal should not be to suppress prices. The goal should be to protect vulnerable households while allowing prices to continue performing their essential role: coordinating decisions, revealing scarcity, and guiding responsible consumption," Dr Ferlito said.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Americans don't think Trump has explained Iran war goals, poll shows
Article:
WASHINGTON: Two out of three Americans think President Donald Trump has not clearly explained why the country went to war with Iran, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll completed on Monday that also showed his approval rating ticking up from the lowest level of his term.
The four-day poll revealed deep concerns about surging gasoline prices, and also suggested many voters are casting blame for their troubles on Trump's Republican allies who will be defending their congressional majorities in the November midterm elections.
More than two months into a conflict that began Feb 28 with a US-Israeli bombing campaign, some 66 per cent of poll respondents - including one in three Republicans and almost all Democrats - said Trump has not "clearly explained the goals of US military involvement in Iran."
The war, which cooled in recent weeks as both sides floated peace proposals, has driven a roughly 50 per cent increase in gasoline prices across the country.
Iran shut down a fifth of the global oil trade by effectively closing the Strait of Hormuz - despite efforts by US warships to re-open the waterway for oil tankers.
Some 63 per cent of the country say their household's personal financial situation has taken a hit from recent gas price increases, up from 55 per cent in a Reuters/Ipsos poll conducted March 17-19.
Some 36 per cent of Americans approve of Trump's performance, up two percentage points since a late April Reuters/Ipsos poll showed his approval rating at 34 per cent, which was the lowest level of Trump's current term in office.
The Reuters/Ipsos poll had a 3-percentage-point margin of error in either direction based on the number of people surveyed.
Trump's popularity remains below the 40 per cent approval rate he had just before the war started.
He started his term in January 2025 with 47 per cent approval after winning the 2024 presidential election on promises to lower costs for Americans.
Three-quarters of the public - including half of Republicans - think his administration bears at least a fair amount of responsibility for the gas price surge, the latest Reuters/Ipsos poll showed.
Asked which political party is more responsible, 65 per cent of poll respondents said Republicans were to blame compared to 27 per cent who said Democrats.
Four out of five Americans said they expect gas prices to rise further. Republicans are defending narrow majorities in the US House of Representatives and the Senate in the midterms.
Their hopes of keeping control of the House have been bolstered by recent court rulings that could lead to voting district boundaries that are more favourable to Republicans.
Republican strategists say the party's chances would further improve if gasoline prices came down.
But with no agreement in sight between Washington and Teheran, about three in 10 Americans already expect to cut back on summer vacation plans if gas prices hold firm, the poll found.
Many expect to cancel their trips or travel shorter distances. Trump has repeatedly promised gas prices will fall when the war ends, though analysts warn that is unlikely to happen quickly.
The public isn't sure who has the upper hand in the conflict. Only one in three say America has the advantage, while about one in seven say Iran has it, the Reuters/Ipsos poll showed.
The rest said they weren't sure or that neither side has an advantage.
The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted online and gathered responses from 1,254 US adults nationwide.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Preacher acquitted of raping 14-year-old girl five years ago
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: The Sessions Court today acquitted and discharged independent preacher Muhammad Fathi Na'im Mazlam of raping a 14-year-old girl at a hotel five years ago.
Judge Tasnim Abu Bakar ruled that the prosecution had failed to establish a prima facie case against the accused at the end of its case.
Fathi Na'im, who was dressed in a black suit, appeared calm in the dock when the decision was delivered.
Tasnim, in her ruling, said the court found the testimony of the victim, who was the third prosecution witness, was not credible.
She said the court had allowed a defence application to challenge the witness's credibility during cross-examination.
"The basis of the defence application was that there were material contradictions in the victim's answers during examination-in-chief compared with her testimony in the accused's trial at the Klang Sessions Court involving the same offence," she said.
Tasnim said the court had heard, examined and considered the testimony of the prosecution witnesses, oral and written submissions by both parties, as well as authorities cited, including the Evidence of Child Witness (Amendment) Act 2024.
"This court finds that the explanations given during the credibility challenge proceedings and throughout the trial could not be categorised as reliable.
"Therefore, the prosecution has failed to establish a prima facie case against the amended charge.
"As such, the accused is discharged and acquitted," she said.
On Dec 1, 2022, Fathi Na'im was charged with raping the girl at a hotel between 2am and 10am in December 2021.
On Nov 29, the same year, the accused was also slapped with nine counts of rape, sexual assault, and sexual intercourse with an object involving the same girl before Klang Sessions Court judge Noridah Adam.
Noridah had sentenced him to 15 years in jail and three strokes of the cane after finding him guilty of two rape charges and one sexual assault charge on Sept 18, last year.
The remaining six charges have yet to begin.
The Klang High Court allowed him a stay of execution pending appeal.
Deputy public prosecutor Siti Pervinah Yusof prosecuted, while lawyer Ashraff Diah appeared for the accused.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: [UPDATED] Light aircraft makes emergency landing in Langkawi, two safe
Article:
PUTRAJAYA: Two people escaped unharmed after a light aircraft made an emergency landing near Kuah, Langkawi, today.
The Civil Aviation Authority of Malaysia (CAAM) said the incident involved a Diamond Aircraft DA-40, registration 9M-HMJ, which was on a training flight.
Langkawi district police chief Assistant Commissioner Khairul Azhar Nuruddin said the aircraft was carrying a male instructor and a female trainee.
The instructor, Captain Mohamed Hafiz Iskandar, and cadet Shah Izzati Maisarah Irwan Shah were reported safe without any injuries.
The aircraft had departed Langkawi International Airport for a training flight at 9.54am.
"At about 10.20am, the flight instructor informed the control tower that the aircraft was experiencing engine power issues and needed to make an emergency landing, he said in a statement.
Khairul Azhar said the aircraft successfully landed at Field B of the Langkawi Stadium at 10.27am, although an initial inspection found that the aircraft's nose gear had broken during the landing.
CAAM, in a statement said that the last communication with the Air Traffic Control Tower Langkawi was recorded at 10.29am.
CAAM confirmed that there were two on board and both were safe.
It said the Air Accident Investigation Bureau (AAIB), under the Transport Ministry, will conduct an investigation into the incident in accordance with Part XXVI of the Civil Aviation Regulations 2016.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Don't turn middle class into govt's 'ATM', Lim warns over RON95 subsidy reform
Article:
KUALA LUMPUR: Kepong MP Lim Lip Eng has urged the government to clearly explain the proposed RON95 fuel subsidy reform, warning that the middle class must not become the government's "ATM".
He said if subsidies were to be removed or reduced for higher-income groups, Putrajaya must first disclose who would be affected, how income would be assessed, and whether factors such as living costs, dependants, loans and daily travel needs would be considered.
"The government cannot throw around labels like T20, T15, T10 or T5 and expect people not to panic.
"High income does not mean rich. A household above the national T20 line may still struggle badly in Kuala Lumpur, Selangor or the Klang Valley after paying for housing, car loans, children's education, insurance, elderly parents and daily living costs.
"These are not millionaires. These are ordinary middle-class families working hard just to keep their heads above water," he said in a statement today.
Lim said the government must not focus solely on income while ignoring real-life expenses, adding that the Central Database Hub (PADU) should be properly utilised since RM85.27 million was reportedly spent on its development and implementation.
"If the final decision is still based on broad labels without proper assessment, then what is the point of PADU?" he said.
He said Malaysians were not against reform, but opposed reforms that were unfair, unclear and careless.
The government, he added, must clarify whether subsidy cuts would be based on personal or household income, and whether location, number of children, elderly parents, housing loans, car loans and travel distance would be taken into account.
"If these questions cannot be answered clearly, then the government is not ready to implement the policy," he said.
Lim warned that middle-class families just above the income threshold could become the biggest victims of the policy.
"They earn slightly more on paper, but their burden is still heavy. The government sees figures, but not pressure," he said.
He added that the middle class was already grappling with high taxes, rising living costs, housing pressure and education expenses.
"Do not turn the middle class into the government's ATM.
"People who work hard, earn more and move up in life should be encouraged, not punished," he said.
Lim also called on the government to immediately disclose the full mechanism, including the income threshold, assessment method, appeal process and transition plan.
"Stop creating fear with unclear standards," he added.
© New Straits Times Press (M) Bhd

Title: Malaysia’s unemployment rate holds steady at 2.9pc in March, says DOSM
Article:
PUTRAJAYA, May 12 — Malaysia’s labour market remained stable in March 2026, with the unemployment rate unchanged at 2.9 per cent, supported by continued expansion in employment and labour force participation, according to the Department of Statistics Malaysia (DOSM).
Chief Statistician Datuk Seri Dr Mohd Uzir Mahidin said the positive economic outlook during the month enabled the country’s labour market to sustain its steady growth momentum.
“The labour force increased by 0.1 per cent to 17.31 million persons in March 2026 from 17.30 million in February, while the labour force participation rate (LFPR) remained at 70.9 per cent,” he said in a statement today.
Mohd Uzir said the number of employed persons also edged up by 0.1 per cent to 16.80 million persons compared with 16.79 million recorded in the previous month.
The DOSM’s Statistics of the Labour Force, Malaysia, March and First Quarter 2026 also show that the number of unemployed persons rose slightly by 0.4 per cent to 509,000 persons from 506,800 in February, leaving the unemployment rate unchanged at 2.9 per cent.
The chief statistician said employees continued to make up the largest share of total employment at 75 per cent, increasing marginally to 12.60 million persons in March from 12.59 million previously.
“On the same note, the number of own-account workers recorded a 0.3 per cent rise to 3.14 million persons compared with 3.13 million in February,” he added.
Mohd Uzir said the services sector remained the main contributor to employment growth, particularly in accommodation and food and beverage services, information and communication activities, as well as transportation and storage activities.
“The manufacturing, construction and agriculture sectors also recorded increases in employment, while the mining and quarrying sector saw a slight decline,” he added.
On unemployment, Mohd Uzir said actively unemployed persons, defined as those available for work and actively seeking jobs, accounted for 79.7 per cent of total unemployed persons. This category increased by 0.3 per cent to 405,800 persons in March from 404,700 in February.
At the state level, Mohd Uzir said the Federal Territory of Putrajaya recorded the lowest unemployment rate at 1.3 per cent, followed by Pahang at 1.9 per cent, while Selangor and Melaka each posted two per cent.
He said Putrajaya also registered the highest LFPR at 79.2 per cent, followed by Selangor at 78 per cent and the Federal Territory of Kuala Lumpur at 76 per cent.
Looking ahead, Mohd Uzir said Malaysia’s labour market is expected to remain stable, supported by strong domestic fundamentals and ongoing structural transformation.
“However, growth is likely to be more moderate and increasingly shaped by external developments, particularly the trajectory of the global energy crisis and geopolitical conditions.
“As such, both businesses and the workforce will need to remain agile in navigating a more dynamic and uncertain economic environment,” he said. — Bernama

Title: Woman, 76, duped out of RM327,600 by scammers posing as police officers
Article:
KUANTAN, May 12 — A 76-year-old woman lost RM327,600 after falling victim to a phone scam syndicate posing as police officers.
Pahang police chief Datuk Seri Yahaya Othman said the victim received a call from an individual posing as a police officer from Sarawak, who claimed that her identity card number had been linked to fraudulent activities and that her bank account was under investigation.
“The suspect also told the victim not to inform her family members or anyone close to her about the matter. She was then instructed to transfer money, purportedly for police investigation purposes, to prove she was innocent, with the assurance that the funds would be returned once the investigation was completed,” he said in a statement today.
Yahaya said the victim made 42 transactions totalling RM327,600 into seven different accounts between May 28, 2025, and March 25 this year, using her savings and loans from moneylending companies.
He said the suspect also instructed the woman to use two land titles as collateral for loans from a moneylending company.
“The victim only realised she had been scammed when her child asked for the land titles to process the inheritance following her husband’s death, as the land was still registered under her late husband’s name,” he said.
Yahaya urged the public to remain vigilant against phone calls from individuals claiming to be enforcement officers or government agencies, and to avoid transferring money to unfamiliar accounts. — Bernama

Title: Utility works may have triggered Wangsa Maju sinkhole, says KL mayor; area closed two weeks for repairs
Article:
